body_excerpt:

meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Lund Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Lund Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. 
Hauberg-2; Hauberg Collection-502; Malmer-14.801.1603; Bruun-1394; Weight: 0.85 gms. Moneyer: Alfward. A stun-
ning example with a razor sharp strike on a completely even flan. 
This excessively rare type is especially interesting because the obverse with the four-lined horizontal legend is one of the 
earliest examples of a Danish type being inspired by Byzantine issues such as the Miliaresia from the late 10th century. The 
reverse was also used in combination with the Nordic serpent type.
€4,000

tremely rare type imitating the Æthelred II’s “ Agnus Dei” type. 
The contemporary hole is a clear indication that this particular religious type with the Lamb of God and the Holy Dove held 
a very special position and was not considered a mere coin but rather an amulet.

meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-1042). Aalborg Mint. Har -
body_excerpt:

thacnut (Hardeknud). NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hau-
berg-39; Hauberg Collection-570; Bruun-1478; Weight: 0.73

meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-1047). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-1047). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC 
Unc Details—Bent. Hauberg-38; Hauberg Collection-727; C. J. Becker, NNUM, 1983, p. 42 ff.; Bruun-1632; Weight: 
0.74 gms. Moneyer: Iule. Apart from light blotches of verdigris, a remarkably lovely piece. Not only is this an extremely 
rare type, it is also highly unusual. The obverse design with the king being shown en face and the fortress on the reverse 
are quite unique, and the reverse legend where the moneyer lets the coin speak is equally remarkable: +IVLE ME FECIT 
(“Jule had me made”).

body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-16; 
Hauberg Collection-910; Bruun-1712; Weight: 0.89 gms. Moneyer: Wulho. Excellent details and considerable traces of 
luster remaining.

Varangians who served as personal bodyguards to the emperor in Constantinople. The present type is derived from the 
Solidi struck by Constantine VII Porpyrogenitus (913 - 959) showing the emperor being crowned by Christ. Although not 
mention

body_excerpt:

tridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-26; Hau-
berg Collection-; Bruun-1798; Weight: 0.76 gms. Moneyer: Seg-

cross, Hauberg-28 (begun during the late 1050s).

examples struck for Erik Ejegod in private hands with its clean strike and a lovely toning with blue and red notes that 
enhances the brilliant luster. 
The amusing type seems to be inspired by Heinrich IV’s issues from Goslar depicting two of the lesser-known disciples, Simon 
and Jude, on the reverse.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: DENMARK. Penny, ND (1134-1137). Lund Mint. Eric II (Erik
body_excerpt:

was considered a pious man. Until 2024 when Queen Margrethe II

meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1533. Malmö Mint. NGC MS-62. Gal-
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Skilling, 1533. Malmö Mint. NGC MS-62. Gal-
ster-81; Sieg-1; Schou-2; Bruun-4153. Weight: 1.90 gms. A truly 
spectacular example of this highly interesting and excessively 
rare issue boasting a nice luster and exceptionally well-struck 
details.
Struck by the Council of State (SENATUS REGNI DANIE) be -
tween the death of Frederik I on April 10th 1533 and the outbreak 
of the so-called Count’s Feud (a war of succession) eventually lead-
ing to the victory of Christian III in 1536.  
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech collection (Copenhagen - 1906)

meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Mark Klippe, 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian 
III. NGC AU-55. Galster-92B; Sieg-16; Schou-48; Bruun-4155. 
Weight: 11.05 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Exceptionally 
well-struck. 
This extremely rare klippe was issued as payment for the merce -
nary troops hired by Christian (III) during the war of succession 
between Christian (III) and his cousin Christian II. It was struck 
in Aarhus, where the former mintmaster of Gottorp, Reynold Junge 
had established a new mint, as the elected king needed one within 
the kingdom itself. 
€5,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H.

meta_line: DENMARK. 2 Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. NGC
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. NGC 
AU-58. Galster-97; Sieg-14; Schou-9; Bruun-4186. Weight: 14.96 
gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Although somewhat double-
struck, the present example is vastly superior to what is normally 
seen at auctions. An unusually nice portrait, struck on a sound 
flan with considerable luster remaining.
During the war of succession, the so-called Count’s Feud, the Mint 
had to move with the army, and in August 1535 Reynold Junge 
established a new Mint at the royal court in Roskilde. 
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Gu

minted to pay the German mercenary soldiers in Scania who in -

from a formidable eye-appeal this is a wonderfully desirable example of this excessively rare one-year type. The portrait 
is clearly inspired by the works of the German artist Jacob Binck who was appointed painter to king Christian, and might 
in fact be his work. 
The Royal Flensburg mint was established in 1544 as the former mint in Gottorp had passed to the king’s

meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden) Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC MS-61. Fr-21; 
Hede-1; Sieg-27; Schou-1; Bruun-4418. Weight: 3.76 gms. Mintmaster: Johan of Vilna (Hans de Willers). A lovely Mint 
State Klippe, with bright luster and limited handling.
This klippe was struck in a number of 15,504 specimens to finance expenses in connection with the sudden outbreak of the 
Northern Seven Years’ War. They where rather crudely minted on unevenly cut flans and it is extremely rare to find an ex-
ample this nicely centered and struck on a broad flan.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E.

body_excerpt:

8228; Hede-8B; Sieg-21; Schou-5; Bruun-4422. Weight: 28.76 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. A contemporary owner’s 
mark in the lower reverse field doesn’t detract from the present example being an exceptionally handsome piece. This 
one-year type that quite unusually bears the date in Roman numerals (LXIII) has one of the highest silver fineness 
(0.937) of all Danish coins minted since the early medieval period. 
€1,200-€1,500

body_excerpt:

Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. More than 100 different variations are known of this interesting issue as it 
was in fact minted with the same date between 1563 and 1576. Although the Mark of 1563 is still very common among 
collectors in ordinary condition, it is however a true rarity in a spectacular condition as the present. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Extremely Rare and Lustrous Klippe

meta_line: DENMARK. Gold Krone (Golt Krone) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU Details—Scratches.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Gold Krone (Golt Krone) Klippe, 1564. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU Details—Scratches. 
Fr-23; Hede-5; Sieg-26; Schou-2; Bruun-4487. Weight:3.39 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Köppelin. Some light scratches but 
exceptionally well-struck and lustrous and excessively rare with only a few in private hands. 
This interesting klippe, struck during the Northern Seven Years War, was minted in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm 
in only 600 specimens. The mintmaster, Hans Köppelin was transferred to Flensburg in 1566 where he acted until 1570.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

body_excerpt:

sharply struck details. Originally minted by Hans Köppelin in only 995 examples and today of the utmost rarity.
Presumably struck in the naval anchor forge on Bremerholm next to the royal castle. In 1619 the forge was turned into Hol-
mens Church, which still stands today.

population that the king had to erect gallows around the country in order to threaten the death penalty if anyone refused to 
accept the king’s coin at face value.

etrates the warm toning on this truly grand example of a highly elusive one-year type.

Bruun-4634. Weight: 7.07 gms. A sharply struck and nicely re-toned example of this very unusual type, believed to have 
been struck in celebration of the marriage between the king’s younger sister, Hedvig and the Duke-Elector of Saxony, 
Christian II on September 12th 1602. 
The present example seems to be the only example outside the National Museum of Denmark, which also owns a unique 
example in gold with the weight of 6 Ducats. Of particular heraldic interest is the fact that the cross

body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1628-RD. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-Unlisted; 
KM-111; Hede-28; Sieg-135; Schou-2; Bruun-5540. Weight: 5.88 gms. Privy Mark: RD. Extremely rare one-year type.
Struck by “Borgerskabets Mønt” (The Citizens’ Mint) consisting of the mintmaster Matthias Clausen, Johan Brahm and the 
mayor of Copenhagen, Jacob Michelsen. After less than half a year, their right to produce coins was withdrawn. Although

meta_line: DENMARK. Ducat, 1662. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-101; KM-236; Hede-16B; Sieg-116.2;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Ducat, 1662. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-101; KM-236; Hede-16B; Sieg-116.2; 
Schou-11; Aagaard-31.1; Bruun-6388. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (ob-
verse), Johan Blum (reverse). Very rare in this exceptional condition with both details and luster being way above what 
is normally encountered.
Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade 
in the center of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg.

tremely rare. Highly brilliant and attractive example.
This beautiful gold type clearly mimics Blum’s Taler-type from 1662. The mintmaster and goldsmith Johan Stichmann is 
known to have been in charge of a part of the militia when Copenhagen was unsuccessfully stormed by the Swedes on 11 
February 1659.

DENMARK. 20 Ducats, 1788-DIA/MF . Altona / Poppenbüttel Mint. Christian VII. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. 
Off-strike in gold of Rigsdaler Courant struck for the personal use of the king and the crown prince. Fr-unlisted; KM-
unlisted (cf. 645 in silver); Hede-25; Sieg-19; Schou-7a; FP-E5 and p. 338; Bruun-7894. Weight: 69.22 gms. Mintmaster: 
Michael Flor. Engraver: Daniel Jensen Adzer. The largest of all Danish / Norwegian gold coins ever minted and unde-
niably one of the most desirable of all in the L. E. Bruun collection. A magnific example of a truly imposing piece that 
will become the jewel

meta_line: DENMARK. Dukat, 1791. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-67. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1;
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Dukat, 1791. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-67. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1; 
FP-47.1; Bruun-7910. Weight: 3.48 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. The so-called 
“Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat produced and the only one which gives the exact weight and fineness in the 
design, suiting it for international trade. Exceptionally well preserved.
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

London by either Milton or the Danish engraver Bauert, who was

buying a Boulton Press. The patterns themselves were struck both 
in London and in Copenhagen. Around 1890 L. E. Bruun com -

meta_line: DENMARK. Silver 2 Skilling Pattern, 1811-IC. Copenhagen
body_excerpt:

Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-62. KM-cf. 670; Hede-9; Sieg-4; 
Schou-2a; Bruun-8010. Weight: 4.46 gms. Extremely rare silver 
pattern which very rarely comes on the market; the last was sold

€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: DENMARK. 12 Skilling, 1812-IC. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. 12 Skilling, 1812-IC. Copenhagen Mint. Freder-
ik VI. NGC MS-64 Red. KM-673; Hede-11; Sieg-6.1; Schou-3; 
Bruun-8014. Mintmaster: Johannes Conradsen (IC). Struck on a 
Skilling 1771, KM-616 (note the inflation from 1 to 12 Skilling). 
The condition of this coin is breathtaking; a real showstopper.
The 1771 Skillings were recalled in 1812 and many were restruck 
as 12 Skillings. On this piece it is especially visible on the obverse.  
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: DENMARK. Lauenburg. 2/3 Taler (Zweidrittel), 1830-FA/FF.
body_excerpt:
DENMARK. Lauenburg. 2/3 Taler (Zweidrittel), 1830-FA/FF. 
Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-61. KM-25; Hede-1; Sieg-
44; Schou-3; Bruun-8082. Mintmaster: Johan Friedrich Freund. 
Engraver: Frederik Alsing. Although minted for use in Lauen -
burg (Lauenborg) this type was struck in Altona in a number of 
only 4,051 pieces. Excellent Mint State example of the only Dan-
ish emission from the small duchy on the borders of Schleswig-
Holstein and Mecklenburg-Schwerin.
Lauenburg ended up in Danish hands after the Napoleonic Wars. 
As part of the agreement on ceding Norway to Sweden, Denmark 
got Swed

body_excerpt:

body_excerpt:

the last Norwegian Archbishop, Olav Engelbrektsson. 
Following his defeat against the Danish protestant king, Christian

valuable treasures from the Nidaros Cathedral.

sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and 
gold coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt

meta_line: SWEDEN. Penny, ND (ca. 995-1022). Sigtuna Mint. Olof Skötkonung. NGC AU-58. Delzanno-24; Llt-13; Malm-
body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Penny, ND (ca. 995-1022). Sigtuna Mint. Olof Skötkonung. NGC AU-58. Delzanno-24; Llt-13; Malm-
er-4.17.56; SMH-30; Bruun-11139. Weight: 2.20 gms. Moneyer: Godwine. Bust right, holding scepter, ‘+OLAFA REX 
ANCOL ’ / Short Cross, ‘C-R-U-X’ in angles, ‘+GODWINE MOTAOIAS’ . An old, dusty toning covers the uncirculated 
surfaces of this the oldest coin type of Sweden. This is the very first Swedish coin in the Bruun Catalogue, and the im-
portance of its provenance cannot be overstated. 
The name of the moneyer, Godwine can also be found on the earliest Norwegian coinage, that of Olav Tryg

(and Ethelred’s) Long Cross Coinage. Bust left, ‘ ANVND REX IN’ / Long Cross, ‘VLAH IN SITVN’ . Excessively rare. 
Astounding condition with a natural, old toning over the uncirculated surfaces. The tiny die striations are perfectly pre-
served as are all the minute details. Three peck marks on the obverse are the only things to reveal this coin was ever in

meta_line: SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1465-1467). Stockholm Mint. Interregnum. NGC AU-55. Delzanno-369; Frösell-224; Llt-1;
body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1465-1467). Stockholm Mint. Interregnum. NGC AU-55. Delzanno-369; Frösell-224; Llt-1; 
Bruun Auction-222; Bruun-11283. Weight: 1.42 gms. Excessively rare. Variation with large cross on obverse. A breath-
takingly beautiful example, lustrous and nearly ‘as struck’ . All three sub-types of the Interregnum coinage are very rare 
in any condition but impossibly so as well-preserved as this. The two examples sold in Sweden in 2009 and 2019 were 
either bent and brushed or spotted with planchet defects, putting this AU example in stark contrast.

body_excerpt:

un Auction-254; Bruun-11316a. Weight: 1.32 gms. Excessively rare, this being the variation with ‘K’ on the obverse and 
no stars by its side. Fully legible with pleasing surfaces far above its numeric grade.
Christian I (of Denmark) was chosen by the nobility after they deposed Karl Knutsson Bonde. He quickly became unpopular 
after imposing heavy taxes, and a rebellion broke out in 1463. Karl Knutsson Bonde returned to the throne in 1464. This 
variation of Christian’s Stockholm coinag

body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Örtug, ND (1497-1501). Stockholm Mint. Johan II (Hans). NGC AU-50. Delzanno-424; Frösell-282; Llt-2; 
Bruun-11316q. Weight: 1.37 gms. Extremely rare variation with cross on obverse, possibly only one other known in private 
collections - this being the better example by far of the two. It is well-centered, well-struck, fully legible and has the most 
beautiful toning. It is a coin that will turn heads and attract serious bidders and one that should most certainly be seen in 
person.
Like his father Christian I, Johan III (Hans of Denmark) had only a short period as ruler of Sweden, as

meta_line: SWEDEN. 1/2 Mark, 1536. Stockholm Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. SM-132(124); Delzanno-103;
body_excerpt:

Appelgren-518(1295); Bruun Auction-403; Bruun-11447. Weight: 6.59 gms. Very rare and excellent for type, 1536 was

uniform and the legend clear.

meta_line: SWEDEN. Daler, 1550. Svartsjö Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC AU-55. Dav-8698; SM-170a(163); Delzanno-29; Bruun
body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Daler, 1550. Svartsjö Mint. Gustav Vasa. NGC AU-55. Dav-8698; SM-170a(163); Delzanno-29; Bruun 
Auction-379; Bruun-11490. Weight: 28.92 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansson. Engraver: Erik Olufsson. Among the most 
beautiful of the 16th century’s Swedish coins, with a both delicate and powerful portrait of the king on the obverse and 
of Christ on the reverse. That, and the very high numeric grade for type will surely make for fierce bidding, as this coin 
will be a jewel in any collection.
The Svartsjö Mint was only operational from 1540 to 1550.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection

SWEDEN. Mark, 1593. Stockholm Mint. Sigismund. NGC MS-61. SM-10(8); Delzanno-10; Bruun-11647. Weight: 
7.59 gms. Extremely rare and in superb condition, ‘as struck’ with lustrous surfaces, razor sharp details and a natural, 
slightly mottled toning. In terms of condition and rarity one of the most impressive of the Swedish coins in this sale.

the throne in Catholic Poland from his maternal grandfather. The Swedish Nobility was not keen on having a Catholic king, but

sive minting started in 1628. The higher denominations, however, are all rare and highly sought, especially in as beautiful a 
condi

meta_line: SWEDEN. Mainz. Taler, 1631. Mainz Mint. Gustav II Adolf. NGC EF-45. Dav-4544(Erfurt); KM-51(Erfurt); SB-
body_excerpt:

21C(Erfurt); Delzanno-373; Bruun Auction-930; Bruun-11857. Weight: 29.08 gms. Mintmaster: Daniel Ayrer. Previ -
ously listed under Erfurt, this Taler, which with smaller variations was also minted in, among others, Hildesheim and 
Erfurt, celebrates the Swedish / Protestant victory at Breitenfeld 1631 during the Thirty Y ears’ War. The mintmaster’s 
mark, the Astrological symbol for the planet Mercury, can be seen on the reverse under the date ‘MDCXXXI’ .

meta_line: SWEDEN. Strassburg. Ducat, 1632. Strassburg Mint. Gustav
body_excerpt:
SWEDEN. Strassburg. Ducat, 1632. Strassburg Mint. Gustav 
II Adolf. NGC AU-55. Fr-3311(Stettin Mint); KM-unlisted; SB-
1(unknown mint); SG-89; Hagander-142(unknown mint); Bru-
un Auction-973; Bruun-11885. Weight: 3.44 gms. Variation with 
‘FINLN’ . Most appealing, with bright luster and sharp details.
Previously linked to Stettin (Szczecin), recent studies now indi -
cate this coinage was struck in Strassburg (French: Strasbourg) 
in 1633/34 as tribute to the Swedish army. The crossed pokers 
mintmark of the mintmaster of Strassburg, Johan Hermann, can 
be seen on the obverse under the shield.

body_excerpt:

body_excerpt:

The Bishopric of Bremen & Verden were ceded to Sweden at the Westphalian Peace in 1648 and turned into a hereditary 
dukedom. Since the Hanseatic city of Bremen itself was separate from the bishopric, the Bishopric Mint was placed in the 
town of Stade. In 1712 the now dukedom was conquered by the Danes an

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich.
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1683/78-HR. Eutin Mint. August Friedrich. 
NGC MS-62. Dav-5442; KM-63; Lange-504c; Bruun-15028. Weight: 29.56 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Ridder. Extremely 
rare Taler in excellent condition and of the highest historical importance to match its Mint State grade. The number 
‘3325’ written ink on the obverse refers to its number in ‘Madai’s Thaler-Cabinett’ from 1765.
Listed in Bruun’s catalogue as 1688 but appears to be 1683 over 1678.
€3,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Ducat, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
body_excerpt:

MS-62. Fr-1508; KM- 80.2; Lange-510AAb; Bruun-15044. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Johan Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. Excessively rare Ducat, only 50 struck, and missing from the auction circuit for decades if not 
longer. Even Lange only had a galvanic copy in his collection. A magnificent looking piece with full luster and lots of 
eye-appeal, this coin is the perfect combination of beauty, rarity and conservation.
€15,000-€25,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

meta_line: GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp. Lübeck (Bishopric). Taler, 1724. Eutin Mint. Christian August. NGC 
MS-62. Dav-2409; KM-79; Lange-511; Bruun-15046. Weight: 29.22 gms. Mintmaster: Johann Justus Jaster. Engraver: 
Ehrenreich Hannibal. A great rarity in incredible condition.
The Younger brother of Friedrich IV of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp, Christian Albert was given the small duchy of Eutin 
and was elected Bishop of Lübeck in 1706. He served as co-ruler in Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp from the death of his brother 
until the coming-of-age of his nephew, Karl Friedrich, in 1717.

meta_line: GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
body_excerpt:
GERMANY . Oldenburg. Lübeck (Bishopric). 5 Taler (Pistole - Friederich Augustd’ or), 1776-B. Altona Mint. Fried-
rich August. NGC MS-61. Fr-1509 and 1931; KM-87 (Lübeck); Trippler/Kalvelage-369; Lange-522; Bruun-15142. 
Weight: 6.66 gms. Mintmaster: Casper Heinrich Lyng. Engraver: Johann Ephraim Bauert. Very rare, only 580 struck; 
200 in 1776 and a further 380 in 1781 using the same dies, making any distinction impossible. Superb condition, the 
second highest ranking on the NGC census.
